era_context: Late Bronze Age collapse period (c. 1200–900 BCE) is still centuries ahead. The mid-second millennium BCE is dominated by the New Kingdom of Egypt (18th–20th Dynasties), the Hittite Empire in Anatolia, Mycenaean Greece, the Shang Dynasty in China, and the Vedic period in South Asia. Regional trade networks and diplomatic correspondence (Amarna Letters era is ~150 years past) connect major powers. This is a period of relative stability before the upheavals of the Late Bronze Age.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (no written records for this period)

events.title: Reign of Thutmose III (Egypt) — mid-reign consolidation
events.description: Thutmose III, one of Egypt's greatest pharaohs, is in the middle of his long reign (c. 1479–1425 BCE). By this point, he has already conducted multiple military campaigns in the Levant and consolidated Egyptian control over Nubia and Syria-Palestine. His administrative reforms and military innovations are reshaping the structure of the New Kingdom. This year falls within his period of maximum territorial expansion and diplomatic influence.

sources.name: Karnak Temple inscriptions and reliefs documenting Thutmose III's campaigns
Historiographic consensus on New Kingdom chronology

events.certainty_note: Thutmose III's reign dates are well-established through Egyptian king lists and astronomical data, though absolute dating of individual years within his reign carries ±5–10 year uncertainty.

events.title: Shang Dynasty consolidation in China
events.description: The Shang Dynasty (c. 1600–1046 BCE) is in its mature phase. Oracle bone inscriptions from this period document divination practices, royal genealogies, and administrative activities. The Shang court is centered in the Yellow River valley, with multiple capitals over time. This year falls within the period of Shang cultural and political consolidation, though specific events for 1946 BCE are not individually attested.

sources.name: Oracle bone inscriptions (jiaguwen) from Yinxu and other Shang sites
Archaeological evidence from Shang capitals (Zhengzhou, Anyang phases)

events.certainty_note: Shang chronology is debated; traditional dates place the dynasty c. 1600–1046 BCE, but some scholars argue for later dates. Individual years within the Shang period are not precisely datable from surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are attested for 1946 BCE in surviving sources. The broader Shang chronology remains contested among scholars; some propose dates 100–200 years later than traditional estimates. Egyptian New Kingdom chronology is more secure but still carries ±5–10 year uncertainty for individual years within Thutmose III's reign.
historiographic_note: For 1946 BCE, documentation is extremely sparse. Egyptian sources (temple inscriptions, administrative records) are the most reliable, but they do not typically record specific events by individual year. Chinese oracle bones provide contemporary evidence but are difficult to date precisely. No contemporary written records survive from most other regions. The historical record for this year is essentially a reconstruction of broader era context rather than specific events.

graph_edges.note: Thutmose III's reign spans multiple decades; this year is part of his broader consolidation period
